pages.title: Important Notice
pages.content:
﻿# Important Notice: Final Test Docker Evaluation

The MVAA 2026 challenge has entered the final hidden-test Docker evaluation stage.

Participants must submit a Docker image reference through this CodaBench portal. Direct upload of prediction masks is **not** used for the official final ranking.

## Required Submission

Each team should submit a `submission.zip` containing a root-level `submission.json` file:

```json
{
  "image": "registry.example.com/mvaa/team001@sha256:xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx",
  "timeout_seconds": 21600
}
```

The Docker image must contain the complete inference environment, including model weights, code, runtime dependencies, and an automatic entrypoint.

## Hidden Test Evaluation

After a descriptor is accepted, a private organizer-side CodaBench worker forwards it to the MVAA GPU runner. The runner pulls the Docker image, runs it on the hidden test inputs, computes official DSC/HD/ASD metrics using the private reference annotations, and returns the scores to this CodaBench leaderboard.

The hidden test images and labels are not released to participants. Labels are never mounted into participant containers.

## Hardware Compatibility

The final evaluation server uses an NVIDIA Tesla V100 32GB GPU. Submitted images must be compatible with compute capability `sm_70`.

## Deadline

The final Docker submission deadline is **August 01, 2026, 23:59 (UTC+8)**.

pages.title: Overview
pages.content:
﻿
# MVAA 2026 Final Test ## Docker-Based Hidden Test Evaluation for Mitral Valve Anatomy Analysis
Mitral valve assessment plays a central role in the diagnosis, treatment planning, and intraoperative guidance of structural heart disease. The MVAA challenge evaluates automated methods for mitral valve anatomy analysis across three imaging modalities: cardiac CT, 3D transesophageal echocardiography (3D TEE), and surgical video frames. This CodaBench portal is dedicated to the **final hidden-test stage**. Participants submit Docker images containing their complete inference pipelines. The organizers then run the submitted images on the hidden test set and compute the official metrics using the private reference annotations. ## Final Test Objective The final test phase evaluates whether submitted algorithms can generalize to unseen data under a standardized computing environment. The evaluation covers: - Task 1: cardiac CT segmentation; - Task 2: 3D TEE multi-class segmentation; - Task 3: surgical video frame segmentation. ## Submission Mode For this stage, participants submit a lightweight `submission.zip` containing only a Docker image reference. The Docker image itself must be hosted in an accessible registry and must include all inference code, model weights, and dependencies. Please read the **Final Docker Submission** page before submitting. ## Resources - Challenge website: https://mwm2026.github.io/mvaa - Baseline code: https://github.com/db0725/MVAA - CodaBench platform: https://www.codabench.org/ - Contact: dbd2002@foxmail.com

pages.title: Final Docker Submission
pages.content:
# Final Docker Submission

This page describes the required Docker submission format for the MVAA 2026 hidden test phase.

During the final test phase, participants do **not** upload prediction masks directly. Instead, each team submits a lightweight `submission.zip` containing a Docker image reference. A private organizer-side CodaBench worker will forward the descriptor to the MVAA GPU runner. The runner pulls the image, runs it on the hidden test set, computes official metrics, and returns the scores to CodaBench.

## 1. What to Submit to CodaBench

Submit a single `submission.zip` file with the following structure:

```text
submission.zip
`-- submission.json
```

The root-level `submission.json` must contain:

```json
{
  "image": "registry.example.com/mvaa/team001:final",
  "timeout_seconds": 21600
}
```

Fields:

- `image`: Docker/OCI image reference containing your inference code, model weights, dependencies, and default entrypoint.
- `timeout_seconds`: Optional runtime limit requested by the participant. The default limit is 21600 seconds (6 hours). The hard maximum is 43200 seconds (12 hours).


Privacy note: the submitted Docker image reference is used only for organizer-side evaluation and is not shown in the public leaderboard or detailed result page. Do not put registry passwords or access tokens in `submission.json`. If your image is hosted in a private registry, please make sure the organizers have pull access before submission.

For reproducibility, a digest-pinned image is recommended:

```json
{
  "image": "registry.example.com/mvaa/team001@sha256:xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx"
}
```


## 2. Reference Baseline Docker Image

The organizers provide a public reference baseline Docker image so participants can inspect the expected Docker-based inference style:

```bash
docker pull db0725/mvaa-baseline-infer:final-20260626
```

This image is provided only as a reference example. Participants may build their own images using any compatible environment, as long as the final image follows the input/output contract described below.

## 3. Docker Image Requirements

Your Docker image must include:

- all inference code;
- all model weights and checkpoints;
- all Python/system dependencies required at runtime;
- a default `ENTRYPOINT` or `CMD` that starts inference automatically.

Your Docker image must **not** require:

- internet access at runtime;
- interactive terminal input;
- downloading extra model weights during evaluation;
- access to hidden labels or organizer-only files.

The submitted image must be a Linux `amd64` Docker/OCI image.

## 4. Organizer Runtime Contract

The organizer runner will execute the submitted image with the following input/output contract. The exact Docker flags may vary by organizer-side infrastructure:

```bash
docker run --rm --gpus all \
  --network none \
  --memory 28g \
  --cpus 14 \
  --pids-limit 2048 \
  --shm-size 8g \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  -v /hidden/test_data:/input:ro \
  -v /job/output:/output:rw \
  -v /job/work:/work:rw \
  -e MVAA_INPUT_DIR=/input \
  -e MVAA_OUTPUT_DIR=/output \
  -w /work \
  YOUR_IMAGE
```

Your container must:

- read test inputs from `/input`;
- write predictions to `/output`;
- exit with code `0` on success.

## 5. Expected Output Layout

After inference, `/output` should contain:

```text
/output/
|-- t1_ct/
|   |-- task1_predictions.json
|   |-- case001_pred.nii.gz
|   `-- ...
|-- t2_tee/
|   |-- task2_predictions.json
|   |-- case001_pred.nii.gz
|   `-- ...
`-- t3_vid/
    |-- task3_predictions.json
    |-- REC_xxx/frame001_pred.png
    `-- ...
```

Task 1 prediction JSON:

```json
{
  "cases": [
    {
      "case_id": "case001",
      "segmentation": "case001_pred.nii.gz"
    }
  ]
}
```

Task 2 prediction JSON:

```json
{
  "cases": [
    {
      "case_id": "case001",
      "segmentation": "case001_pred.nii.gz"
    }
  ]
}
```

Task 3 prediction JSON:

```json
{
  "cases": [
    {
      "case_id": "REC_xxx_000001",
      "segmentation": "REC_xxx/REC_xxx_000001_pred.png"
    }
  ]
}
```

The `segmentation` field must be a relative path under the corresponding task output folder. Absolute paths and path traversal such as `../` are not allowed.

## 6. Mask Format

Task 1:

- File format: `.nii.gz`
- Labels: `0` for background, `1` for target foreground.

Task 2:

- File format: `.nii.gz`
- Labels: `0` for background, `1` and `2` for the two target classes.

Task 3:

- Recommended file format: `.png`
- Labels: `0` for background, any positive value for foreground. Binary masks using `255` for foreground are recommended.

## 7. GPU Compatibility

The official final-test server uses:

```text
GPU: NVIDIA Tesla V100 32GB
CUDA driver: 550.x / CUDA 12.4
Compute capability: sm_70
```

Your Docker image must be compatible with NVIDIA V100 / `sm_70`. Do not rely on Ampere/Ada/Hopper-only features such as BF16-only execution, TF32-only kernels, or CUDA extensions compiled only for `sm80`, `sm86`, or `sm89`.

If you compile custom CUDA extensions, include:

```bash
TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST="7.0"
```

## 8. Minimal Dockerfile Example

```dockerfile
FROM pytorch/pytorch:2.5.1-cuda12.4-cudnn9-runtime

ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1
ENV MVAA_INPUT_DIR=/input
ENV MVAA_OUTPUT_DIR=/output

WORKDIR /workspace

COPY requirements.txt /workspace/requirements.txt
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r /workspace/requirements.txt

COPY src/ /workspace/src/
COPY weights/ /workspace/weights/
COPY run.sh /workspace/run.sh
RUN chmod +x /workspace/run.sh

ENTRYPOINT ["/workspace/run.sh"]
```

Example `run.sh`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

INPUT_DIR="${MVAA_INPUT_DIR:-/input}"
OUTPUT_DIR="${MVAA_OUTPUT_DIR:-/output}"

mkdir -p "${OUTPUT_DIR}/t1_ct" "${OUTPUT_DIR}/t2_tee" "${OUTPUT_DIR}/t3_vid"

python /workspace/src/infer.py \
  --input "${INPUT_DIR}" \
  --output "${OUTPUT_DIR}"
```

## 9. Local Test Command

Before final submission, test your image locally:

```bash
docker run --rm --gpus all \
  --network none \
  -v /path/to/test_input:/input:ro \
  -v /path/to/output:/output:rw \
  your-image:final
```

Then package `submission.json`:

```bash
zip submission.zip submission.json
```

pages.title: Data Access Method
pages.content:
# Data Access Method

## Training and Validation Data

Participants should use the released training and validation data for algorithm development and local validation.

### Option 1: Baiduyunpan

- Link: https://pan.baidu.com/s/1LYCH_MtTgqpiTUQsn0ToNQ?pwd=3ymi
- Password: `3ymi`

### Option 2: Google Drive

Both links are required for the complete released dataset:

- Link 1: https://drive.google.com/file/d/14WneBUBZ1X4p69tRdRzximNb0IsWuh2B/view?usp=sharing
- Link 2: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1K4vFo-4pdL1_xt-8msO-32bc2aEdJ7wS/view?usp=sharing

## Hidden Test Data

The hidden test set is not distributed to participants. During final evaluation, the organizers mount the hidden test inputs into each submitted Docker container at:

```text
/input
```

The hidden test labels are only accessible to the organizer-side scorer and are never mounted into participant containers.

## Baseline Code and Reference Docker Image

Participants can access the official baseline and documentation here:

- https://github.com/db0725/MVAA

The baseline is intended as a reference implementation. A public reference Docker image is also available for participants who want to inspect the expected Docker-based inference style:

```bash
docker pull db0725/mvaa-baseline-infer:final-20260626
```

Final submissions must follow the Docker format described in the **Final Docker Submission** page.

## Task 3 Training Annotation Note

One Task 3 video training sample may contain an annotation issue:

```text
t3_vid/train/REC_20250322_101917_746A/REC_20250322_101917_746A_000130.png
```

Participants may choose to exclude this sample during model training or data cleaning. This issue is limited to the listed training sample; the validation and hidden test annotations are not affected.

pages.title: Core Task
pages.content:
# Core Task

The MVAA challenge is designed to reflect the real-world clinical workflow across multiple imaging modalities. The challenge is structured into three primary segmentation tasks.

### Participation and Model Submission Requirements

Participants are required to complete and submit results for all three tasks. The official final ranking will be determined using the normalized final score described on the **Final Ranking** page; therefore, submissions for only one or two tasks will not be sufficient for the final ranking.

However, participants are not required to use a single unified multimodal model. Each task may be addressed independently, and participants may train and submit separate models for the CT, 3D TEE, and surgical video frame segmentation tasks.

### Task 1: Mitral Valve Segmentation in Cardiac CT
This task focuses on the automated, multi-class anatomical segmentation of the mitral valve from 3D cardiac CT scans.
*   **Target:** Participants are required to segment the mitral annulus and the anterior/posterior leaflets.
*   **Clinical Significance:** High-resolution CT segmentation provides the precise spatial geometry necessary for pre-procedural planning, including device sizing and the selection of repair strategies for interventions like TEER or annuloplasty.

### Task 2: Mitral Valve Segmentation in 3D Transesophageal Echocardiography (3D TEE)
This task addresses the segmentation of valve structures from volumetric 3D TEE (ultrasound) data, which serves as the key intraoperative reference for valve function.
*   **Target:** Delineation of the valve leaflets from 3D ultrasound volumes.
*   **Technical Focus:** Algorithms must be robust enough to handle ultrasound-specific artifacts, acoustic noise, signal dropout, and ambiguous boundaries between the leaflets and surrounding cardiac tissues.

### Task 3: Mitral Valve Segmentation from Surgical Video Frames
This task focuses on extracting anatomical information from individual frames extracted from intraoperative surgical videos.
*   **Target:** Pixel-level segmentation of the mitral valve structures as seen from the surgeon's direct viewpoint.
*   **Methodology:** Although the data originates from video recordings, this task is defined as a frame-based segmentation problem. Participants should treat the provided frames as individual 2D images for training and inference.
*   **Clinical Focus:** This task enables the verification of leaflet motion and structural continuity during the actual intervention, providing real-time feedback within the surgical environment.

pages.title: Dataset
pages.content:
# MVAA Challenge - Dataset System

## 1. Ethics Approval & Data License

### 1.1 Ethics Approval
All data is anonymized, with approval from the Medical Ethics Committee (No. KY-N-2022-048-01).

### 1.2 Data Usage Agreement & License
* **License:** CC BY-NC (Attribution-NonCommercial)
* No commercial use of the data is permitted; all usage must comply with the license terms.

## 2. Data Source

### 2.1 Acquisition Devices
* **Cardiac CT:** Siemens Healthineers, GE Healthcare, Philips Healthcare, and Canon Medical Systems.
* **3D TEE:** Philips Epiq cardiac ultrasound system.
* **Surgical Video:** Standard endoscopic video recorder (720p, 30fps).

### 2.2 Acquisition Centers
The dataset is collected from multiple clinical centers, including:
* Guangdong Provincial People’s Hospital
* The First Affiliated Hospital of Jinan University
* Dongguan Eastern Central Hospital

## 3. Dataset Scale & Partition

### 3.1 Task 1: Cardiac CT Segmentation
* **Training Set:** 1,067 images total (27 with ground-truth labels, 1,040 unlabeled for semi-supervised learning).
* **Validation Set:** 30 images (without labels).

### 3.2 Task 2: 3D TEE Segmentation
* **Training Set:** 105 images with ground-truth labels.
* **Validation Set:** 20 images (without labels).

### 3.3 Task 3: Surgical Frame Segmentation

* **Training Set:** 1,559 frames total.
    * **Labeled Data:** 180 frames (118 frames contain the mitral valve).
    * **Unlabeled Data:** 1,379 frames (for semi-supervised learning).
* **Validation Set:** 48 images (without labels, all containing mitral valve structures for scoring).

#### 3.3.1 Task 3 Label Note

Task 3 labels may include annotations beyond the mitral valve. Only the mitral valve label is used for training and evaluation. In this task, the mitral valve is the only target segmentation class.

Participants should use the `*_Label.nii.gz` file within each `*_Label.tar` archive as the source of ground-truth annotations, and refer to the category mapping table in the corresponding `*_Label.json` file for confirmation. Specifically, category ID `10` corresponds to the mitral valve.

The `*_Label.json` file is intended only for reading category names, colors, and metadata, and should not be used as the primary label source for training.

Some polygon annotations in the JSON files are incomplete or empty; therefore, generating training labels from `Polys.Shapes` is not recommended. Of the 180 labeled frames, 118 contain valid mitral valve annotations. Unlabeled frames may also include images without visible mitral valve structures.


## 4. Data Novelty
* **Multi-modal Data Resource:** The challenge includes cardiac CT, 3D TEE, and surgical video data collected from multiple clinical centers. In the current public release, these modalities are provided as independent datasets, and no formal CT-video pairing metadata or case mapping table is included.
* **Unpublished Data:** More than 140 newly collected, unpublished ultrasound images have been provided for validation and testing to ensure fair evaluation of generalization.

## 5. Inclusion & Exclusion Criteria

### 5.1 Inclusion Criteria
1. Standard clinical cardiac views suitable for mitral valve assessment.
2. Sufficient image quality for expert annotation of leaflets and annulus.
3. Availability of corresponding clinical measurement references.

### 5.2 Exclusion Criteria
1. Severe imaging artifacts or signal dropout hindering anatomical visibility.
2. Duplicated or corrupted data.
3. Cases with uncertain clinical reference measurements.

## 6. Annotation Characteristics

### 6.1 Annotation Method
* **Two-Stage Process:** Initial annotations by two trained medical experts followed by review and refinement by a board-certified radiologist with over 10 years of experience.
* **Semi-automated refinement:** For ultrasound data, an initial automatic segmentation was followed by manual review and modification by two annotators until agreement was reached.

### 6.2 Annotation Quality Control (QC)
* **Inter-observer Reliability:** Evaluated on a subset of 30 images using ICC and absolute error distributions.
* **Adjudication:** Senior clinical specialists resolved discrepancies to ensure the reliability of reference annotations.

## 7. Data Preprocessing
1. **Anonymization:** Removal of all patient-related information from metadata.
2. **Standardization:** CT and TEE data converted to NIfTI format; surgical video frames extracted as 2D images.
3. **Resampling:** Data resampled to a consistent resolution to ensure uniform slice-level annotation.

## 8. Main Annotation Error Sources
1. **Inter-Annotator Variability:** Differences in identifying subtle mitral valve boundaries in low-contrast cases.
2. **Annotation Ambiguity:** Inherent ambiguity of valve boundaries due to pathological alterations or imaging limitations.
3. **Technical Limitations:** Errors introduced by the resolution constraints of the annotation tools.

pages.title: Evaluation
pages.content:
# Evaluation

## Final Test Evaluation Mode

In the final hidden-test phase, participants submit Docker image descriptors through CodaBench. A private organizer-side CodaBench worker forwards accepted descriptors to the MVAA GPU runner, which pulls each submitted image, runs inference on the hidden test set, computes official metrics, and returns the resulting scores to the CodaBench leaderboard.

Metrics are computed outside the public CodaBench environment on the organizer-side server. The leaderboard columns in this portal are the official hidden-test raw metrics returned by that runner. The official overall ranking is based on normalized metric scores as described on the **Final Ranking** page.

## Metrics

All tasks are evaluated using Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC), Hausdorff Distance (HD), and Average Surface Distance (ASD). Higher DSC is better; lower HD and ASD are better.

### Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)

DSC measures overlap between the predicted segmentation and the ground truth. It is computed as twice the intersection divided by the sum of the predicted and reference foreground sizes.

### Hausdorff Distance (HD)

HD measures the largest bidirectional surface discrepancy between the prediction and reference mask. Surfaces are extracted from binary masks and Euclidean distances are computed using image spacing when spacing metadata is available.

### Average Surface Distance (ASD)

ASD is the symmetric mean surface-to-surface distance between prediction and reference masks.

## Task-Specific Computation

### Task 1: CT Segmentation

- Foreground label: `1`
- Per-case metrics: DSC, HD, ASD on the binary foreground mask
- Final task score: mean across all Task 1 cases

### Task 2: TEE Segmentation

- Foreground labels: `1` and `2`
- Metrics are computed separately for each foreground class and averaged within each case
- Final task score: mean across all Task 2 cases

### Task 3: Video/Image Segmentation

- Prediction foreground: any positive pixel value
- Reference foreground: the case-specific `target_label`
- Final task score: mean across all Task 3 frames/cases

## Evaluation Notes

- HD and ASD use physical spacing when available; otherwise unit spacing is used.
- Missing or invalid predictions receive penalty metrics: `DSC = 0`, `HD = large penalty`, `ASD = large penalty`.
- If both prediction and reference are empty for an evaluated class, that class is treated as a perfect match: `DSC = 1`, `HD = 0`, `ASD = 0`.
- Prediction paths in JSON files must be relative paths under the corresponding task output folder. Absolute paths and `../` traversal are rejected.

## Leaderboard Columns

The final leaderboard reports:

- `Task1_DSC`, `Task1_HD`, `Task1_ASD`
- `Task2_DSC`, `Task2_HD`, `Task2_ASD`
- `Task3_DSC`, `Task3_HD`, `Task3_ASD`
- `Evaluation_Status`
- `Runtime_Seconds`

`Evaluation_Status = 1` means the organizer-side runner completed successfully. `Evaluation_Status = 0` means descriptor validation, image pulling, inference, or scoring failed; failed submissions receive penalty metrics.

## Participant Result Summary

After a successful final-test submission, participants will see the leaderboard values for the three mean metrics of each task. Per-case or per-image scores are not shown in the final hidden-test phase.

The detailed result summary follows this structure:

```text
MVAA Final Hidden-Test Evaluation
Status: done
Message: official hidden-test evaluation completed

Task-level mean metrics:
- Task 1 (CT): mean DSC = ..., mean HD = ..., mean ASD = ...
- Task 2 (TEE): mean DSC = ..., mean HD = ..., mean ASD = ...
- Task 3 (Video/Image): mean DSC = ..., mean HD = ..., mean ASD = ...

Evaluation_Status: 1
Runtime_Seconds: ...
```

pages.title: Final Ranking
pages.content:
# Final Score, Prizes, and Certificates


The official overall ranking will be based on normalized metric scores from the final hidden-test evaluation. The raw leaderboard metrics will remain visible for transparency, while the normalized score will be used for final ranking and award decisions.

Because Task 2 uses a public dataset, its three normalized metric scores will be assigned as 100 points for all eligible teams to avoid unfair advantage from public test-set information.

To be eligible for prize money, a team must satisfy the participation rules, rank in the overall top 10, and, barring exceptional circumstances approved by the organizers, must attend the onsite presentation.

In addition to the overall awards, the top three teams in Task 1 and the top three teams in Task 3 will receive additional task-specific certificates.

pages.title: Participation Rules
pages.content:
# MVAA Challenge - Participation Rules

## 1. Challenge Life Cycle

The final test stage uses hidden data and Docker-based evaluation. The official ranking is determined by running each accepted Docker submission on the hidden test set and applying the normalized final score described on the **Final Ranking** page.

## 2. Participation Policies

### 2.1 Algorithm Development

- Task 1 and Task 3 encourage semi-supervised learning using the released labeled and unlabeled data.
- Task 2 is a supervised 3D TEE segmentation task.
- Public external datasets and public pre-trained models are allowed.
- Private datasets, private annotations of public data, and hidden test data leakage are strictly prohibited.

### 2.2 Organizer Institute Participants

Members of the organizers' institutes may participate but are not eligible for awards and will not be listed in the official award ranking.

## 3. Final Docker Submission Rules

### 3.1 Submission Method

Participants must submit a `submission.zip` file containing a root-level `submission.json` with a Docker image reference.

Direct prediction-mask uploads are not used for final hidden-test ranking.

### 3.2 Docker Image Requirements

The Docker image must include:

- inference code;
- model weights;
- all runtime dependencies;
- a default entrypoint that runs inference automatically.

The Docker image must not require internet access during evaluation. Runtime network access may be restricted or disabled by the organizers.

### 3.3 Runtime Environment

The organizer-side runner will provide:

- `/input`: hidden test inputs, read-only;
- `/output`: prediction output directory, writable;
- one NVIDIA Tesla V100 32GB GPU;
- runtime network access may be restricted or disabled.

### 3.4 Submission Limits

Each team may submit up to 10 times in total during the final test phase. Among them, at most 7 valid submissions are allowed for each team.

The additional 3 submission attempts are reserved for possible technical failures, such as descriptor-format errors, image-pulling failures, platform errors, or other unexpected submission issues. These failed or invalid attempts may be excluded from the valid-submission count at the organizers' discretion.

The submission limit applies to the team as a whole, not to individual CodaBench accounts. Submissions made from different accounts belonging to the same team will be counted together. If members of the same team use multiple accounts to bypass the submission limit, the team may be disqualified.

This rule is intended to ensure fairness for smaller teams.

## 4. Award Policy

1. The official overall ranking is based on the normalized final score described on the **Final Ranking** page.
2. A total prize pool of 10,000 RMB will be awarded to eligible teams in the overall top 10 after score verification and rule-compliance checks.
3. To receive prize money, an eligible top-10 team must attend the onsite presentation unless exceptional circumstances are approved by the organizers.
4. In addition to the overall awards, the top three teams in Task 1 and the top three teams in Task 3 will receive additional task-specific certificates.
5. Outstanding teams may be encouraged to submit extended versions of their methods to a related journal special issue.
6. Eligible top teams may be invited to contribute to the challenge summary paper.

## 5. Result Announcement

Official final results will be announced after the organizers have completed hidden-test execution, score verification, and rule-compliance checks.

## 6. Publication Policy

Participating teams may publish their methods separately, while respecting challenge data usage rules and collective challenge contributions.

## 7. Code Availability Requirement

Algorithm code release through a public GitHub repository and the submission of a corresponding conference paper are prerequisites for award eligibility.

pages.title: Timeline
pages.content:
﻿# Timeline

All deadlines are at **23:59 (UTC+8)** on the dates listed below. Dates may be adjusted depending on the official MICCAI 2026 schedule.

## Key Milestones

- **April 15, 2026:** Training Data Release
  - Participants can download the released training datasets.

- **June 01, 2026:** Validation Data Release
  - The validation leaderboard opens for preliminary scoring.

- **July 01, 2026:** Final Test Docker Submission Opens
  - Participants prepare and submit Docker image descriptors through this CodaBench portal.

- **August 01, 2026:** Final Docker Submission Deadline
  - Deadline for all final Docker image descriptors.

- **August 2026:** Hidden-Test Execution and Score Verification
  - The organizers run accepted Docker images on the hidden test set and verify official scores.

- **September 01, 2026:** Winner Announcement and Invitations
  - Top-performing teams are announced and invited to present.

- **September 27 - October 01, 2026:** MICCAI 2026 Conference
  - The official challenge workshop, final result presentation, and award ceremony will take place during MICCAI 2026 in France.

## Note

Any schedule changes will be announced through the competition website and email notifications to registered participants.

## 4. Role of Clinical Experts
* Provide real-world clinical data (CT, 3D TEE, and Surgical Video) acquired under routine diagnostic conditions across multiple institutions and patient populations.
* Perform and supervise expert annotations, including pixel-level segmentation mask labeling in accordance with standardized clinical protocols.
* Evaluate annotation quality and inter-rater consistency to ensure that the reference labels accurately reflect true clinical variability and meet established diagnostic standards.

pages.title: Technical Support
pages.content:
﻿# MVAA Challenge - Technical Support

## 1. Platform Role

This CodaBench portal collects final Docker submission descriptors and returns official hidden-test scores. The descriptor is first validated by CodaBench, then a private organizer-side CodaBench worker forwards the Docker image reference to the MVAA GPU runner.

## 2. Final Evaluation Server

The official final-test runner uses a standardized GPU server:

```text
GPU: NVIDIA Tesla V100 32GB
CUDA driver: 550.x / CUDA 12.4
Compute capability: sm_70
Runtime network inside participant container: disabled
```

Participant containers are run with `/input` mounted read-only and `/output` mounted writable.

## 3. Recommended Local Validation

Before submitting, participants should test their image locally:

```bash
docker run --rm --gpus all \
  --network none \
  -v /path/to/test_input:/input:ro \
  -v /path/to/output:/output:rw \
  your-image:final
```

The container should exit with code `0` and write prediction JSON files and masks under `/output`.

## 4. Common Failure Reasons

- `submission.json` is not at the root of `submission.zip`.
- The `image` field is missing or contains whitespace/control characters.
- The Docker image is private and the organizers cannot pull it.
- The Docker image requires internet access at runtime.
- The container does not have an automatic entrypoint.
- The image is not compatible with NVIDIA V100 / `sm_70`.
- Prediction files are missing or paths in prediction JSON contain absolute paths or `../`.
- The container exceeds the configured runtime, memory, or disk limits.

## 5. Technical Contact

For technical issues regarding Docker submission, data integrity, or evaluation scripts, please contact:

- Technical Lead: [tyt6xx@163.com](mailto:tyt6xx@163.com)
- Challenge Contact: [dbd2002@foxmail.com](mailto:dbd2002@foxmail.com)

pages.title: Future Plans
pages.content:
# MVAA Challenge - Publication & Future Plans

The MVAA challenge has a comprehensive publication framework, with a focus on clinical relevance, methodological novelty, and long-term multimodal benchmark development.

## 1. Conference Proceedings Publication
* **Springer LNCS:** We will coordinate post-conference proceedings, enabling participating teams to publish detailed method descriptions in the **Springer Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS)** volumes linked to the 1st MICCAI Workshop on Medical World Models (MWM).
* **Open Access:** This mechanism ensures that all successful technical contributions are publicly available and citeable within the MICCAI ecosystem.

## 2. Journal Special Issue Submission
* **Outstanding Contributions:** High-impact submissions will be invited to submit extended manuscripts to the **IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering (TBME)** Special Issue entitled: *“Digital Twins, Simulation-Based AI and Medical World Models.”*
* **Selection Criteria:** Evaluation goes beyond leaderboard ranking, focusing on methodological novelty, cross-modal generalization, and real-world clinical deployment potential for mitral valve analysis.

## 3. Challenge Summary Manuscripts
* **Journal-level Summary:** We will coordinate a comprehensive challenge summary manuscript to report and analyze MVAA outcomes.
* **Analysis:** Content will include a meta-analysis of all participating methods, key findings in multimodal segmentation, and research trends in structural heart disease analysis.
* **Purpose:** This paper serves as an authoritative reference for the community, informing future research directions in unified anatomical modeling.

## 4. Long-Term Future Plans
1. **Annual Repeated Event:** Maintain the challenge as an **annual event** with fixed MICCAI deadlines, continuously expanding the dataset with new multimodal clinical data.
2. **Integration with World Models:** Further integrate the benchmark with **medical world models and digital twin frameworks**, extending mitral valve analysis to longitudinal and predictive surgical modeling.
3. **Clinical Translation:** Promote the translation of top-performing algorithms into clinical tools for mitral valve repair, TEER, and annuloplasty, reducing operator dependence and improving intervention safety.
4. **Global Collaboration:** Establish a sustainable consortium for multimodal heart research, fostering ongoing collaboration between clinicians, sonographers, and AI researchers.

pages.title: Q&A
pages.content:
# Q&A

## What should I submit in the final test phase?

Submit `submission.zip` containing a root-level `submission.json` with your Docker image reference.

## Do I upload model weights separately?

No. Your Docker image must include all model weights and dependencies needed for inference.

## Will my Docker container have internet access?

Your image must not require internet access during hidden-test evaluation. Runtime network access may be restricted or disabled by the organizers.

## Where are the hidden test images mounted?

The hidden test inputs are mounted at `/input` inside your container.

## Where should predictions be written?

Write all prediction JSON files and masks under `/output`.

## Does the CodaBench leaderboard show final DSC/HD/ASD scores?

Yes. After descriptor validation, the private organizer-side worker runs the submitted image on the hidden test set and returns official Task1/Task2/Task3 DSC, HD, and ASD metrics to this leaderboard.

## Why might my row show Evaluation_Status = 0?

`Evaluation_Status = 0` means validation, image pulling, container execution, output checking, or scoring failed. Check the submission logs and the `Message` line in the detailed score output.

## Which GPU should my image support?

The official runner uses NVIDIA Tesla V100 32GB. Your image must support compute capability `sm_70`.

## Will my Docker image reference be visible to other participants?

No. The Docker image reference is used for organizer-side evaluation and is not shown in the public leaderboard or detailed result page. If you need to protect code or weights, use a private registry and provide pull access to the organizers.
## Is there a reference baseline Docker image?

Yes. Participants may pull the public reference baseline image to inspect the expected Docker-based inference style:

```bash
docker pull db0725/mvaa-baseline-infer:final-20260626
```

This image is only a reference example. Participants may submit their own Docker images as long as they follow the required input/output contract.

## How many final-test submissions are allowed?

Each team may submit up to 10 times in total during the final test phase. Among them, at most 7 valid submissions are allowed for each team.

The additional 3 submission attempts are reserved for possible technical failures, such as descriptor-format errors, image-pulling failures, platform errors, or other unexpected submission issues. These failed or invalid attempts may be excluded from the valid-submission count at the organizers' discretion.

The limit applies to the team as a whole, not to individual CodaBench accounts. If members of the same team use multiple accounts to bypass the submission limit, the team may be disqualified. This rule is intended to ensure fairness for smaller teams.

A private organizer-side Codabench worker forwards the descriptor to the MVAA GPU runner, which pulls the image, runs inference on the hidden test set, computes official metrics, and returns Task1/Task2/Task3 scores to Codabench.
